title: Minimum Efficient Scale

content.statement: There's a smallest size you have to reach before your cost per unit stops falling.
content.explainer: Fixed costs spread thinner as volume grows, so cost-per-unit drops until it flattens at the minimum efficient scale. Operate below that point and you're structurally more expensive than rivals who cleared it, no matter how hard you work.
questions: Do you actually know the volume at which your cost-per-unit stops dropping, or are you guessing?
Are you competing on price against someone who has already crossed a scale you haven't reached?
Which of your fixed costs are so large that only high volume can ever make them cheap per unit?
sop: Separate your costs into fixed (unchanged by volume) and variable (rising with each unit).
Model unit cost across a range of volumes using the fixed and variable figures (see calculation, page 6).
Find the volume where unit cost stops falling meaningfully, your minimum efficient scale.
Compare your current volume against that scale to see if you're structurally sub-scale.
Decide to grow to the scale, restructure fixed costs down, or exit segments where you can't compete.

definitions.def: The lowest output volume at which cost-per-unit reaches its minimum; below it you are more expensive per unit than a larger competitor.
A cost that stays the same regardless of how many units you produce, such as rent, equipment, or core salaries.
A cost that rises with each additional unit produced, such as materials or shipping.

content.equation: Unit Cost = (Fixed Cost ÷ Volume) + Variable Cost
content.equation_example: At 10,000 units: ($100,000 ÷ 10,000) + $5 = $10 + $5 = $15 per unit. At 50,000 units: ($100,000 ÷ 50,000) + $5 = $2 + $5 = $7 per unit. Higher volume drives unit cost toward the $5 variable floor; low volume leaves fixed costs bloating every unit.
content.closing_statement: Knowing the scale a game demands tells you honestly whether it's yours to play.
content.question: Do you actually know the volume at which your cost-per-unit stops dropping, or are you guessing?

caption:
Principle Nº 345: Minimum Efficient Scale

There's a smallest size you have to reach before your cost per unit stops falling.

Ask yourself: Do you actually know the volume at which your cost-per-unit stops dropping, or are you guessing?
